records.ety: [OE. wagen, OF. wagier, gagier, to pledge, promise, F. gager to wager, lay, bet, fr. LL. wadium a pledge; of Teutonic origin; cf. Goth. wadi a pledge, gawadjōn to pledge, akin to E. wed, G. wette a wager. See Wed, and cf. Gage.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Waged (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Waging (?).]

defs: To pledge; to hazard on the event of a contest; to stake; to bet, to lay; to wager; as, to wage a dollar.
quotes: My life I never but as a pawn To wage against thy enemies.

defs: To expose one's self to, as a risk; to incur, as a danger; to venture; to hazard.
quotes: To wake and wage a danger profitless.

defs: To engage in, as a contest, as if by previous gage or pledge; to carry on, as a war.
quotes: [He pondered] which of all his sons was fit To reign and wage immortal war with wit.
The two are waging war, and the one triumphs by the destruction of the other.

defs: To adventure, or lay out, for hire or reward; to hire out.
To put upon wages; to hire; to employ; to pay wages to.
quotes: Abundance of treasure which he had in store, wherewith he might wage soldiers.
I would have them waged for their labor.

defs: To give security for the performance of.
To bind one's self; to engage.

records.ety: [OF. wage, gage, guarantee, engagement. See Wage, v. t. ]

defs: That which is staked or ventured; that for which one incurs risk or danger; prize; gage.
That for which one labors; meed; reward; stipulated payment for service performed; hire; pay; compensation; -- at present generally used in the plural. See Wages.
quotes: By Tom Thumb, a fairy page, He sent it, and doth him engage, By promise of a mighty wage, It secretly to carry.
Existing legislation on the subject of wages.
notes: ☞ Wage is used adjectively and as the first part of compounds which are usually self-explaining; as, wage worker, or wage-worker; wage-earner, etc.
